front: Empire: its capital city was reportedly laid out in the shape of a puma, with the city's tail formed by two rivers
Empire: called itself Tawantinsuyu, the "Land of the Four Quarters," and was divided into four suyus that converged on its capital
Empire: kept records on knotted-string devices called quipu (khipu), later burned en masse by Spanish colonial authorities
Empire: stretched along the Andes from roughly Ecuador to Chile and used Quechua as its administrative language
Event: the sun god Inti sent this figure, accompanied by his sister/wife Mama Ocllo, to found a city wherever a golden staff sank into the ground -- the future Cuzco
work: Origins and the Founding of Cuzco

front: Event: this legendary founder emerged with three other "Ayar Brothers" and four sisters from a cave at Tambo Toco, becoming sole founder after his brothers were lost along the way (one grew wings and flew off, another was sealed in a cave)
work: Origins and the Founding of Cuzco

front: Institution: the first person to hold this imperial title was believed to be the son of the sun god Inti, and was traditionally Manco Capac
work: Origins and the Founding of Cuzco

front: Ruler: repelled a Chanka invasion of Cuzco -- legend holds stones turned into soldiers to help him -- after his father or brother fled the besieged city, then seized the royal fringe from his brother Urco
Ruler: took the name meaning "Earthshaker" or "he who overturns/reverses the world"
Ruler: transformed the Kingdom of Cuzco into the Inca Empire, reorganizing it into Tawantinsuyu and reportedly introducing the hanan/hurin social classes and the mitma resettlement policy
Ruler: built the Coricancha ("Golden Enclosure") sun temple, commissioning life-sized gold statues of animals and plants for its garden
Ruler: began the conquest of the Chimu people, work later completed by his son Topa Inca Yupanqui
Play: its title general rebels after being forbidden from marrying Pachacuti's daughter Cusi Coyllur
Institution: organized labor and land through kinship groups called this, whose local head, the curaca, divvied up land and labor
Institution: required rotational labor service for public works such as roads, storehouses, and temples, rather than monetary taxation
Institution: this resettlement policy forcibly relocated conquered populations into new regions, replacing them with loyal subjects, and moved an estimated 25-30% of the empire's population
Institution: after an emperor's death, his mummy and property were maintained by this kind of royal kin group, which also controlled private armies and fueled succession disputes
Institution: girls called this were selected to weave and brew chicha beer in state and religious service, and were expected to remain celibate before marriage
Institution: preserved food by freeze-drying, producing chuno from potatoes and ch'arki (source of the English word "jerky") from meat, stored in hillside storehouses called qollqa
Institution: used the terraced site of Moray as an agricultural testing ground, part of a system of exploiting multiple ecological zones that scholar John Victor Murra called a "vertical archipelago"
Institution: this roughly 25,000-mile Incan road network linked Quito to Mendoza (through Chile) via the capital Cuzco, and was built and maintained through mit'a labor
Institution: relay runners along the Inca road system carried messages and quipu, resting at inns spaced about a day's walk apart and blowing horns called pututus to signal their approach
Institution: the Inca road system crossed gorges with suspension bridges woven from ichu grass -- rebuilt annually and still standing in the village of Huinchiri -- or with basket-and-cable crossings
Institution: the road system's most-clued surviving stretch, near Machu Picchu, passes a mountain pass whose name translates to "dead woman"
Institution: the Inca road system was marked with ritual cairns called apacheta and connected sacred shrine sites called wak'a
War: broke out between the half-brothers Huascar and Atahualpa after their father and his intended heir both died in a smallpox epidemic
Ruler: reportedly dreamed of thousands of foreigners standing on an empty battlefield shortly before his death, which precipitated a civil war between his two sons
War: Atahualpa's generals Chalcuchimac and Quizquiz won this war for him; the defeated enemy general Atoc reportedly had his skull turned into a drinking cup and his skin made into a drum
War: Huascar's side temporarily captured Atahualpa's forces at Tumebamba after the Canari people defected to Huascar, before ultimately losing this war

Battle: at this 1532 engagement, friar Vicente de Valverde offered Atahualpa a Bible, which he threw to the ground, giving the Spanish a pretext to ambush and capture him despite being outnumbered roughly forty to one
work: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the Empire

front: Ruler: offered to fill a room once with gold and twice with silver as ransom for his freedom, but was executed anyway in 1533, reportedly after ordering the killing of his brother Huascar
work: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the Empire

front: Ruler: installed as a puppet emperor, he was imprisoned by Francisco Pizarro's brothers, escaped, and besieged Cuzco for ten months before retreating to found a rump state in 1537
work: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the Empire

front: Event: this rump Inca state was the last holdout after the empire's fall; its final ruler, Tupac Amaru, was captured and executed when the Spanish conquered it in 1572
work: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the Empire

front: Institution: this imperial title, meaning roughly "the only emperor," came with a braided crown of gold tubes and a scarlet fringe, and was represented in art by four black feathers
work: The Sapa Inca and State Religion

front: Institution: this sun god was believed to be the ancestor of the Sapa Inca and chief deity of the empire; his wife/sister was the moon goddess Mama Quilla
work: The Sapa Inca and State Religion

front: Institution: this sun temple at Cuzco, whose name means "Golden Enclosure," was tended by the Virgins of the Sun, who guarded its sacred fire
work: The Sapa Inca and State Religion

front: Institution: this ritual sacrificed chosen children at high-altitude shrines, producing frozen mummies later known as "La Doncella" and "Mummy Juanita"
work: The Sapa Inca and State Religion

front: Institution: this annual purification ("spring cleaning") festival at Cuzco had special hymns written for it by the ruler Pachacuti
work: The Sapa Inca and State Religion

front: Work: this illustrated, roughly 1,400-page letter to the Spanish king denounced Spanish mistreatment of the Inca; it was written by Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala
back: The First New Chronicle and Good Government

front: Work: written by Garcilaso de la Vega, son of a Spanish conquistador and an Inca noblewoman, and partly composed in Quechua
back: Royal Commentaries of the Incas (Comentarios Reales)

front: Concept: anthropologist John Victor Murra coined this term for the empire's system of exploiting multiple ecological zones/altitudes for agriculture
Work: a painting depicting the ruler's capture appears on the cover of this book, which controversially attributes the empire's fall largely to Old World technological and disease advantages
